title: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: Opus 4.6 independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO). Economic Prospect Score: 70/100 — Strong Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y10)
fcfGrowth.rationale: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. is projected to grow FCF at 9% annually, reflecting its competitive position, sector dynamics, and historical compounding rate.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 9.0% WACC balances Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.'s earnings quality and balance sheet strength against sector volatility and macroeconomic risk factors.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 3.0% terminal rate assumes long-run growth converges toward nominal GDP, appropriate for a mature S&P 500 constituent.

thesisTitle: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.: A Fundamental Assessment
thesisParagraphs: Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (TMO) is a large-cap constituent of the S&P 500 with established market position and consistent cash flow generation. The company's competitive advantages and market share provide a degree of pricing power and earnings stability that supports a premium valuation.

My DCF applies a 9% FCF growth rate reflecting the company's historical compounding ability and forward outlook, with a 9.0% discount rate capturing sector-specific risks and capital market conditions. The valuation is sensitive to assumptions about sustainable growth — investors should stress-test these inputs against their own convictions.
verdictFooter: Opus 4.6 independent analysis. Data via public filings (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: What is Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.'s intrinsic value according to Opus?
faq.answer: Based on a 10-year DCF with 9% FCF growth and 9.0% WACC, Opus calculates TMO's intrinsic value at approximately $529.22 per share, suggesting the stock is fairly valued at $580.00.

faq.question: What assumptions drive TMO's valuation?
faq.answer: The key drivers are the FCF growth rate (9%) and discount rate (9.0%). A 1% increase in growth adds roughly 10-15% to the intrinsic value. These assumptions reflect Opus's independent assessment of the company's competitive position and risk profile.

faq.question: Should I buy or sell based on this analysis?
faq.answer: This is not financial advice. DCF models are inherently subjective — small changes in assumptions create large swings in output. Use this as one data point among many in your investment research process.

prospectVerdictDetail: The picks-and-shovels play for life sciences — dominant in lab equipment, reagents, and CRO services. Post-pandemic destocking is ending.
prospectKeyRisks: Biotech funding downturn reducing instrument and consumable demand
China anti-corruption campaign reducing life science spending in Asia
Post-pandemic destocking cycle extending longer than expected
prospectKeyCatalysts: Biotech funding recovery driving instrument demand rebound
Bioproduction and cell/gene therapy tools growing 15%+
AI-driven drug discovery increasing demand for analytical instruments
prospectMethodology: Score is based on three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30), totaling 0-100. Each pillar is broken into individually scored factors with transparent rationale. Data sources include FY2025 10-K filings, analyst consensus estimates, news sentiment analysis, and competitive landscape assessment. The score is forward-looking and represents economic prospect over a 2-3 year horizon.
